schools.summary: Ellicott dates the counsel after the Hebron coronation and parses the captains, hundreds (cf. Num. 31:14), and princes as the Great Council; Keil and Delitzsch set the Chronicler's religious aim of restoring the Levitic worship over against Samuel's political interest.
Gill, following Lightfoot, sets the consultation at the coronation, its execution delayed past Hiram's embassy, the taking of Jerusalem, and the Philistine wars.
Henry makes the consultation a pattern of pious rule: 'What pious thing shall I do?' — a king who consults rather than commands, calls the people brethren, and owns Saul's neglect as his own.
Clarke: having secured Zion, his army, and Israel's goodwill, David's first step for religion was to fetch the ark from its obscure village to the royal city.

themes: the ark to the royal city

schools.summary: Keil takes 'all Israel' from the south frontier to the north, present by thirty thousand representatives (2 Sam. 6:1); Ellicott parses the independent clause 'if it be from Jehovah,' the late-Hebrew idioms, and the Levites' pasture-cities.

Wesley frames the chronology: the assembly dismissed, the Philistine wars intervening (2 Sam. 5:17; 1 Chron. 14:8), then a second assembly called; 'that it be of the LORD' = if the removal please God.
Gill: David addressed the officers as representatives of the whole congregation.

Ellicott reads 'bring round' (as the throne passed to David, 1 Chron. 10:14; 12:23), the ark's twenty-year neglect at Kirjath-jearim (1 Sam. 7:2), and 'we sought Him not' — doubting the ark was ever an oracle (Exod. 25:10-22; 1 Kings 8:9).
Gill: the ark as the symbol of the divine Presence, which David longed above all to set at the head of his reign.
Wesley: the people had contented themselves with sacrificing at Gibeon, careless of the ark, the soul of the tabernacle; David used his new power for religion first.

Henry weighs the unanimity: resolved nemine contradicente; leaders of good works find readier concurrence than they expect, and the people's own consent makes it a national act fit for a national blessing.
Ellicott renders 'So should we do' (cf. 1 Chron. 5:5; 9:25), 'the thing' being the king's whole proposal.
Gill: the congregation agreed to the motion with one mind.

Keil narrates the whole ascent from Baalah; Ellicott sets the frontiers, reading 'Shihor' as the border Wady el Arish (Isa. 27:12; 2 Chron. 7:8; Josh. 13:3) though granting it means the Nile in Isa. 23:3, and Hamath as the northern bound (Amos 6:2).
Clarke, with the Targum, reads 'from the Nile of Egypt to the entrance of Antioch' — the great river, not the border torrent.
Henry: worth travelling far to attend the ark; a cause of joy at the revival of neglected ordinances — though even wise and good men then fall into mistakes.
Gill: 'all Israel' means the thirty thousand chosen men.

debates.question: What is the southern landmark 'Shihor of Egypt'?

sides.position: the border torrent (Wady el Arish, the River of Egypt) on the Sinai frontier
the Nile itself, 'from the Nile even to the entrance of Antioch'

schools.summary: Ellicott prefers Samuel's 'Nachon' over 'Chidon,' calls 'put forth his hand to hold' a paraphrase of Samuel's barer text, and renders 'the oxen plunged' (the verb of casting down, 2 Kings 9:33).
Henry: the sin of Uzza warns against presumption and irreverence with holy things — a good intention does not justify a bad action.
Clarke refers the transaction to his fuller note at 2 Sam. 6:6.

Ellicott identifies Baalah/Kirjath-baal (Josh. 15:60), renders 'Jehovah who sitteth upon the cherubim' (Ps. 80:2; Isa. 37:16), and reads 'whose name is called on it' through the later reverence for the Name (Lev. 24:16), suspecting a lost 'upon it' (cf. 2 Sam. 6:2; 1 Chron. 15:3); Keil carries the verse in the narrative.
Clarke, with the Targum, reads 'where his name is invoked' — the Name invoked at the ark, not called upon it.

themes: the LORD enthroned on the cherubim
the Name called on the ark

schools.summary: Henry calls the cart a grave error: David copied the Philistines (1 Sam. 6:12), but we must walk by rule, not by example — the ark was for the Levites' shoulders, not oxen.
Clarke reads the new cart as reverence — new lest the ark be profaned by a common carriage — and, with Jarchi, takes Uzza and Ahio for Abinadab's sons (2 Sam. 6:3).
Ellicott finds the verse an abridgment of 2 Sam. 6:3 that omits 6:4, and notes 'Ahio' may be pointed 'his brothers.'

debates.question: How should the ark's carriage on a new cart be judged?

sides.position: a mark of reverence — a new, unused cart lest the ark be profaned
a grave error — copying the Philistines against the rule that the Levites must bear it on their shoulders

schools.summary: Henry: welcome the ark as a guest none shall lose by; though to Uzza a savour of death, to the reverent it is a savour of life (2 Cor. 2:16).
Ellicott: 'by the house,' 'in its own house'; the blessing seen in Obed-edom's numerous sons (1 Chron. 26:4-8) and the peace of those who trust the LORD (Ps. 125).
Clarke reports the Targum's marvel of fourscore-and-one born in a day, disavowing it while letting it witness the household's prospering.

themes: judgment and mercy from one presence

schools.summary: Keil carries the six kinds of music of the joyful ascent; Ellicott glosses 'played' as 'were dancing,' resolves Samuel's 'cypress-wood' crux, notes the Chronicler's later term for cymbals, and reads 'before God' as 'before the ark.'

Ellicott notes the abridgment of 'God smote him,' the obscure Hebrew behind 'because he put his hand,' and the parallel stroke on Beth-shemesh (1 Sam. 6:19); Keil carries the blow through to David's displeasure.
Henry: like Nadab and Abihu, Uzza's death proclaims that God will be sanctified in those who draw near (Lev. 10:3) — serve the LORD with fear even in gladness.
Wesley: the case warns against presumption; a right intention does not justify a wrong action.

themes: God sanctified in those who draw near

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'made a breach' = 'broke forth against' (as at 1 Chron. 14:11; Exod. 19:22); 'unto this day' is the Chronicler's transcript of his source, not a claim for his own time.
Wesley: 'Perez-uzza' = the breach of Uzza; David's displeasure warns us not to quarrel with God when he reproves us.
Henry: take heed to our spirits under rebuke, and be not driven from duty by providences meant to drive us from sin — David should have gone on.

Ellicott alone comments, chiefly on the text: the Chronicler's reverent 'God' for Samuel's 'Jehovah' (cf. v.8, v.14), the Aramaic 'hek' for 'how,' and 'shall I bring' for Samuel's 'shall come'; David's displeasure turns to holy fear.

themes: the reverent avoidance of the Name
how to bring the ark home

schools.summary: Ellicott: 'David caused not the ark to turn aside unto himself'; Obed-edom a Korhite Levite (1 Chron. 26:1-4), 'Gittite' meaning a native of the Levitical Gath-rimmon (Josh. 21:24), of Kohath charged to bear the ark (Num. 4:15).
Clarke agrees Obed-edom was a Levite, but explains 'Gittite' as a sojourner at Gath (1 Chron. 15:17-18).

sides.position: a Levite who was a sojourner at the Philistine Gath
a native of Gath-rimmon, a Levitical town of Kohath
